title: Datatilsynet breach reporting

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run, then normalised in Phase A (clause 'Personopplysningsloven' → '§8'). Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NO Personopplysningsloven §8 (Processing of special categories of personal data) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.26.16: Datatilsynet breach reporting.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.26.16' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)
spl:
index=privacy sourcetype="breach:ticket" jurisdiction="NO" earliest=-180d
| eval confirmed=strptime(confirmed_at,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval filed=strptime(datatilsynet_filed_at,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval hours=round((filed-confirmed)/3600,2)
| eval breach_sla=if(isnull(filed) OR hours>72,1,0)
| table breach_id, confirmed_at, datatilsynet_filed_at, hours, breach_sla
description: Tracks breach assessment milestones and Datatilsynet notification timestamps for supervisory evidence.
value: Tracks breach assessment milestones and Datatilsynet notification timestamps for supervisory evidence.
implementation: (1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders.
visualization: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux

knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: We track breach assessment milestones and Datatilsynet notification timestamps for supervisory evidence — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=privacy sourcetype="breach:ticket" jurisdiction="NO" earliest=-180d
| eval confirmed=strptime(confirmed_at,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval filed=strptime(datatilsynet_filed_at,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval hours=round((filed-confirmed)/3600,2)
| eval breach_sla=if(isnull(filed) OR hours>72,1,0)
| table breach_id, confirmed_at, datatilsynet_filed_at, hours, breach_sla
```

Understanding this SPL

**Datatilsynet breach reporting (Personopplysningsloven)** — Tracks breach assessment milestones and Datatilsynet notification timestamps for supervisory evidence.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: privacy; **sourcetype**: breach:ticket. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=privacy, sourcetype="breach:ticket", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **confirmed** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **filed** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **hours** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **breach_sla** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Pipeline stage (see **Datatilsynet breach reporting (Personopplysningsloven)**): table breach_id, confirmed_at, datatilsynet_filed_at, hours, breach_sla

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Datatilsynet breach reporting (Personopplysningsloven)** — Tracks breach assessment milestones and Datatilsynet notification timestamps for supervisory evidence.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Change.All_Changes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).
